nativeRange.habitat: Rocky intertidal and subtidal zones on wave-exposed and sheltered coasts
nativeRange.biotope: Temperate rocky shore; encrusts rocks from the mid-intertidal zone into the subtidal

imageCandidates.notes: Product photo from Mosaic Macros listing; sole trade source for this species.

summary: Irish Moss is an iconic North Atlantic cold-water red alga (despite the 'moss' name) best known as the commercial source of carrageenan. Requires cool water (6–18°C) and is generally incompatible with tropical reef aquariums.
careNotes:
Chondrus crispus is a temperate North Atlantic red alga belonging to family Gigartinaceae — not a true moss. It displays a distinctive thin, lobed, fan-branching thallus that tends to appear deep red to purple, with gametophyte frond tips showing a characteristic bluish iridescence under full-spectrum lighting.

CRITICAL TEMPERATURE NOTE: This species is a cold-water organism native to the North Atlantic, where temperatures typically range 6–18°C (43–64°F). It has been reported to decline in tanks exceeding approximately 24°C (75°F). It is not suited for typical tropical reef aquariums (24–27°C). It may be kept in cold-water or species-appropriate marine setups, or in controlled chilled systems. careLevel is set to 'advanced' primarily because of this cold-water requirement, which makes it incompatible with most home reef setups.

This entry is Mosaic Macros only — no AlgaeBarn or Top Shelf Aquatics listing was found. Mosaic offers cuttings in sizes '1–3 inch on plug' and '5 inch+'. The species may require overnight air shipping as it is flagged as not guaranteed hardy on standard 2-day transit.

In the wild, Irish Moss grows on rocky intertidal and subtidal substrates along Atlantic coasts of Europe and North America. It is the primary commercial source of carrageenan (up to 55% dry weight), which is widely used as a food and cosmetic thickener. In aquarium settings, it can attach to live rock or rubble via its holdfast. Nutrient uptake capacity is considered low relative to refugium workhorses such as Chaetomorpha — it is primarily a display specimen.

Propagation in aquaria is typically by fragmentation (cutting thallus sections). The species undergoes a gametophyte–sporophyte alternation of generations in nature, but captive propagation is generally by simple cutting.
breedingNotes: Reproduces in nature via alternation of generations (gametophyte and sporophyte phases are morphologically similar). In captivity, propagation is typically accomplished by fragmentation — cutting sections of the thallus and allowing them to attach or float until holdfast develops. Sexual reproduction in a home aquarium setting has not been documented in commonly available sources.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source found for this species. Confirms species name, cold-water temperature sensitivity (declines above 75°F), and display use. Limited husbandry detail provided.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Chondrus crispus
additional.notes: Cross-verified taxonomy (Gigartinaceae, Gigartinales, Florideophyceae, Rhodophyta), native range (North Atlantic — Ireland, Iceland, Faroe Islands, Baltic to Spain, Atlantic Canada/US), morphology (up to ~20 cm, 2–15 mm wide branches), habitat (rocky intertidal/subtidal), and carrageenan composition. No aquarium-specific parameters; water parameters and care data extrapolated from wild ecology and temperature warning from Mosaic.
